main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (Neural ODEs) for radiation parameterization","Profile-wise RNN (discrete substitution of Neural ODE)","Linking trained neural network to WRF operational weather forecast model"],"problems":["High computational cost of atmospheric radiation parameterization in weather and climate models","Efficient and accurate emulation of radiative transfer (heating rates and radiation fluxes) in the atmosphere"],"datasets":["Atmospheric data modeled with WRF within an Arctic region"],"metrics":["Prediction accuracy of long- and short-wave heating rates","Computational speedup (runtime) compared to conventional radiation parameterization"],"results":[{"metric":"Computational speedup","value":"26.5 times faster radiation steps compared to conventional parameterization"},{"metric":"Prediction accuracy","value":"No significant loss of accuracy compared to conventional parameterization"}],"novelty_claims":["Application and investigation of Neural ODEs for atmospheric radiative transfer parameterization","Demonstration that a profile-wise RNN (discrete Neural ODE) is optimal for this task","Integration of the trained neural network into an operational weather forecast model (WRF) with significant computational speedup and maintained accuracy"]}

methods: Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (Neural ODEs) for radiation parameterization
Profile-wise RNN (discrete substitution of Neural ODE)
Linking trained neural network to WRF operational weather forecast model
problems: High computational cost of atmospheric radiation parameterization in weather and climate models
Efficient and accurate emulation of radiative transfer (heating rates and radiation fluxes) in the atmosphere
datasets: Atmospheric data modeled with WRF within an Arctic region
metrics: Prediction accuracy of long- and short-wave heating rates
Computational speedup (runtime) compared to conventional radiation parameterization

results.value: 26.5 times faster radiation steps compared to conventional parameterization
No significant loss of accuracy compared to conventional parameterization

novelty_claims: Application and investigation of Neural ODEs for atmospheric radiative transfer parameterization
Demonstration that a profile-wise RNN (discrete Neural ODE) is optimal for this task
Integration of the trained neural network into an operational weather forecast model (WRF) with significant computational speedup and maintained accuracy

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Physics-incorporated deep learning framework for radiative transfer","Encoding physical relationship between fluxes and heating rates as a network layer to enforce energy conservation","Comparison of various deep learning architectures: fully connected neural networks (FC NNs), convolutional neural networks (CNNs), bidirectional recurrent neural networks (RNNs), transformer-based neural networks, neural operator networks"],"problems":["High computational cost of atmospheric radiative transfer calculations in numerical weather prediction models","Need for physically consistent and credible deep learning-based parameterizations of radiative transfer"],"datasets":[],"metrics":["Prediction accuracy (unspecified metric)","Offline evaluation performance"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a physics-incorporated deep learning framework for radiative transfer modeling, encoding the physical relationship between fluxes and heating rates as a network layer to enforce energy conservation.","Demonstrates that incorporating physical laws into the deep learning model improves prediction accuracy and physical consistency.","Comprehensively compares multiple deep learning architectures for radiative transfer parameterization, showing the superiority of bidirectional RNNs, transformer-based NNs, and neural operator networks over FC NNs and CNNs."]}

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (Neural ODEs)","Continuous-depth residual networks","Continuous-time latent variable models","Continuous normalizing flows","Adjoint sensitivity method for backpropagation through ODE solvers"],"problems":["Memory inefficiency in training deep neural networks due to storing intermediate activations","Lack of adaptive computation in standard deep networks","Difficulty in modeling continuous-time data with neural networks","Limitations of normalizing flows due to discrete transformations and bottlenecks"],"datasets":[],"metrics":[],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Proposes parameterizing the derivative of the hidden state using a neural network, leading to continuous-depth models solved by ODE solvers.","Introduces a method to backpropagate through any ODE solver without access to its internal operations, enabling constant memory cost for training.","Constructs continuous normalizing flows that can be trained by maximum likelihood without partitioning or ordering data dimensions.","Demonstrates continuous-time latent variable models that can naturally handle data arriving at arbitrary times."]}

novelty_claims: Proposes parameterizing the derivative of the hidden state using a neural network, leading to continuous-depth models solved by ODE solvers.

Demonstrates continuous-time latent variable models that can naturally handle data arriving at arbitrary times.

results.value: MALI achieves new state-of-the-art results

novelty_claims: MALI is the first method to enable feasible training of Neural ODEs on large-scale datasets such as ImageNet.
MALI achieves constant memory cost with respect to integration time, similar to the adjoint method, but also guarantees accuracy in reverse-time trajectory and gradient estimation.
MALI enables better accuracy than a well-tuned ResNet with the same number of parameters on image classification tasks.
MALI achieves new state-of-the-art results for continuous generative models (e.g., FFJORD on MNIST and CIFAR-10).

methods: MTP-GO model: a graph-based probabilistic multi-agent trajectory prediction model using temporal graph neural networks, neural ordinary differential equations (neural ODEs), mixture density networks, and Extended Kalman Filtering (EKF)
Spatio-temporal graph neural network encoding with graph-gated recurrent cells
Neural ODE-based motion modeling for learning dynamic constraints
Mixture density output for multimodal probabilistic prediction
Integration of Extended Kalman Filter for uncertainty estimation
problems: Accurate and robust prediction of future trajectories of multiple interacting road users in traffic scenarios
Capturing multimodal and probabilistic nature of agent behaviors in dynamic environments
Improving interpretability and physical feasibility of deep learning-based trajectory predictions
Handling diverse agent types and their distinct dynamics in multi-agent forecasting
datasets: rounD dataset (as mentioned in the figure caption)
Various naturalistic traffic datasets (not all named explicitly)
metrics: Prediction accuracy (e.g., alignment with ground truth trajectories)
Multimodal prediction capability (ability to represent multiple possible maneuvers)
novelty_claims: Introduction of MTP-GO, a model that combines spatio-temporal graph neural networks with neural ODEs for multi-agent trajectory prediction
Use of graph-gated recurrent cells to preserve inter-agent interactions throughout the prediction process
Adaptation to dynamic environments and consistent forecasting for all agents, even with limited historical data
Learning physically feasible, smooth trajectories via neural ODEs
Probabilistic, multimodal prediction using a combination of mixture density networks and Extended Kalman Filtering
Outperforming several state-of-the-art methods on multiple metrics across various datasets

methods: Learning a space of dynamics spanned by neural ODE basis functions using function encoders and Hilbert space theory
Pre-training basis functions (neural ODEs) on a suite of functions offline
Zero-shot identification of new system dynamics at runtime via efficient inner product calculation (no retraining or gradients required)
Representing system dynamics as a linear combination of pre-trained neural ODE basis functions

Lower than baseline, indicating more efficient control

Demonstrates state-of-the-art system modeling accuracy and improved control efficiency in robotics environments

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["High-fidelity ship performance modeling in ice and open water","Metocean (meteorological and oceanographic) forecasting for sea states and ice conditions","Voyage optimization algorithm for route planning and efficiency/safety trade-offs","Cost analysis procedure integrating operational, fuel, and voyage costs","Holistic approach combining ship performance, weather/ice forecasts, and cost–benefit analysis"],"problems":["Uncertainty in predicting ship speed, voyage time, and fuel costs for Arctic routes due to variable ice and weather conditions","Lack of accurate, realistic cost–benefit analysis for trans-Arctic shipping as an alternative to the Suez Canal Route (SCR)","Insufficient consideration of dynamic ice conditions and ship-specific performance in previous studies","Need for reliable decision-support tools for shipowners considering Arctic routes"],"datasets":["High-fidelity ship performance data for a general cargo vessel (case study)","Metocean forecast data (weather and ice conditions) for the NSR and SCR","Operational cost data for merchant shipping (fuel, voyage, and operational costs)","Historical data on NSR and SCR transits (for context and validation)"],"metrics":["Total voyage cost (including operational, fuel, and voyage costs)","Voyage time (duration)","Fuel consumption","Economic feasibility (cost comparison between NSR and SCR)"],"results":[{"metric":"Total voyage cost (NSR vs SCR, different seasons)","value":"Trans-Arctic route (NSR) can be economically feasible compared to SCR under certain conditions; specific cost values are provided in the case studies (not detailed in the provided excerpt)"},{"metric":"Voyage time (NSR vs SCR)","value":"NSR offers shorter distances and potentially shorter voyage times, but actual times depend on ice and weather conditions (quantitative values in case studies)"},{"metric":"Fuel consumption (NSR vs SCR)","value":"Total fuel consumption is calculated using high-fidelity models, showing realistic expenses for both routes (quantitative values in case studies)"}],"novelty_claims":["First work to consider ice floe resistance in ship performance modeling for Arctic cost–benefit analysis","Integration of high-fidelity ship performance models, metocean forecasts, and a novel voyage optimization tool for realistic route prediction","Holistic, case-based approach providing more accurate and trustworthy cost–benefit analysis for Arctic shipping compared to previous studies relying on statistical or empirical data","Provides a practical decision-support tool for shipowners and operators considering the NSR as an alternative to the Suez Canal"]}

methods: High-fidelity ship performance modeling in ice and open water
Metocean (meteorological and oceanographic) forecasting for sea states and ice conditions
Voyage optimization algorithm for route planning and efficiency/safety trade-offs
Cost analysis procedure integrating operational, fuel, and voyage costs
Holistic approach combining ship performance, weather/ice forecasts, and cost–benefit analysis
problems: Uncertainty in predicting ship speed, voyage time, and fuel costs for Arctic routes due to variable ice and weather conditions
Lack of accurate, realistic cost–benefit analysis for trans-Arctic shipping as an alternative to the Suez Canal Route (SCR)
Insufficient consideration of dynamic ice conditions and ship-specific performance in previous studies
Need for reliable decision-support tools for shipowners considering Arctic routes
datasets: High-fidelity ship performance data for a general cargo vessel (case study)
Metocean forecast data (weather and ice conditions) for the NSR and SCR
Operational cost data for merchant shipping (fuel, voyage, and operational costs)
Historical data on NSR and SCR transits (for context and validation)
metrics: Total voyage cost (including operational, fuel, and voyage costs)
Economic feasibility (cost comparison between NSR and SCR)

results.metric: Total voyage cost (NSR vs SCR, different seasons)
results.value: Trans-Arctic route (NSR) can be economically feasible compared to SCR under certain conditions; specific cost values are provided in the case studies (not detailed in the provided excerpt)
NSR offers shorter distances and potentially shorter voyage times, but actual times depend on ice and weather conditions (quantitative values in case studies)
Total fuel consumption is calculated using high-fidelity models, showing realistic expenses for both routes (quantitative values in case studies)

novelty_claims: First work to consider ice floe resistance in ship performance modeling for Arctic cost–benefit analysis
Integration of high-fidelity ship performance models, metocean forecasts, and a novel voyage optimization tool for realistic route prediction
Holistic, case-based approach providing more accurate and trustworthy cost–benefit analysis for Arctic shipping compared to previous studies relying on statistical or empirical data
Provides a practical decision-support tool for shipowners and operators considering the NSR as an alternative to the Suez Canal
